SUMMARY:
This paper aims to review the challenges and prospects of adopting climate-smart agricultural practices and technologies and their implications for food security in sub-Saharan Africa. To find relevant information about the challenges and opportunities of adopting CSA practices and technologies and its implication for food security, the search keywords like ''climate-smart agriculture'' with ''food security'' and ''challenges and prospects'' with ''CSA″ were combined. et al., [27] | Smart Strategies for Enhanced Agricultural Resilience and Food Security Under a Changing Climate in Sub-Saharan Africa, in Climate Change and Multi-Dimensional Sustainability in African Agriculture: Climate Change and Sustainability in Agriculture | SSA | No single CSA technology and practice offers a solution to the foregoing challenges and must be integrated when choosing the most suitable climate-smart technologies and practices for any given agro-ecology |

Challenges and prospects of adopting climate-smart agricultural practices and technologies: Implications for food security
==========================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Rusha Begna Wakweya

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jafr.2023.100698](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jafr.2023.100698 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2666154323002053&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Key challenges in CSA adoption includes socioeconomic, political, and institutional. 
*   •Opportunities for adopting CSA were access to resources in different forms. 
*   •Reconsidering the socioeconomic, political, and institutional elements is needed. 
*   •Implementation of multidisciplinary approaches is also required. 

Abstract
--------

Climate-smart [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CSA) has become popular as a practical solution for food insecurity in the face of [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This paper aims to review the challenges and prospects of adopting climate-smart agricultural practices and technologies and their implications for [food security](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/food-security "Learn more about food security from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in sub-Saharan Africa. The study used an integrative literature review based on inclusion and exclusion criteria. The result of the review indicated that lack of appropriate policies and political commitment, lack of knowledge of CSA technologies by [smallholder](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/smallholder "Learn more about smallholder from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") farmers, institutional constraints, and financial constraints were identified as the main challenges to the adoption of CSA technologies and practices, while the availability of resources in the form of projects, programs, technologies, practices, and other applications were opportunities for policymakers, advisors, and farmers. Therefore, there is a need for the linkage of farmers, researchers, and extension practitioners, considering appropriate sets of CSA interventions of relevant scale, selected and tailored to local specific contexts and livelihood needs.

Keywords
--------

Climate-smart agriculture

Prospects

Challenges

Agriculture

Food security

1. Introduction
---------------

[Climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about Climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has been affected [smallholder](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/smallholder "Learn more about smallholder from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, threatening their [food security](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/food-security "Learn more about food security from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and livelihoods [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib1)]. There is an increasing decline in agricultural production, declining food accessibility, and financial success in countries whose livelihood depends on farming due to the increased incidence and severity of the famine, changes in climate, and rain-fed [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib2)]. Climate change and other structural and institutional challenges have led to a lack of food in Sub-Saharan Africa [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib3)]. Problems in current agricultural development policies include shrinking agricultural frontiers, declining [soil fertility](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/soil-fertility "Learn more about soil fertility from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), feeding a rapidly growing population [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib4)], and reducing rural poverty [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib5)].

Improving African agriculture requires an integrated approach and a new paradigm to address these interconnected difficulties [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib6)]. Sustainable intensification is a unique path to agricultural transformation in Africa [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib7),[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib8)]. [CSA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/climate-smart-agriculture "Learn more about CSA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a holistic approach that includes context-specific technologies and practices such as sustainable intensification. To address economic and environmental Problems, recent agricultural policies have focused on these techniques [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib9),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib10)]. “CSA approaches that increase production and improve adaptability are an option for sustaining agricultural output [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib11),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib12)]. Today, agriculture that is climate-smart is presented as an exceptional perspective for meeting both [global warming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/global-warming "Learn more about global warming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reduction and adjustment objectives while also ensuring food security [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib13)].Several developing countries, like Ethiopia, have endorsed climate-smart agriculture technologies and practices. Farmer adoption of CSA is hindered by several challenges [[[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib14), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib15)].

CSA is a technique aimed at the improvement of farming methods and the provision of food security in an unstable environment, ignoring the precise context and community-appropriate and flexible outcomes [[[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib16), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib18)]. Identifying desirable practices in this environment and developing a method of rewarding officers who follow these habits could be a critical part of CSA. Although this strategy has a lot of potentials, it is difficult to put into practice due to challenging issues and monitoring costs [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib19)]. According to FAO [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib13)], the novelty of ideas toward the technologies and practices of CSA limited farmer's exposure. Likewise, CSA interventions require a great deal of experience and skill to understand a well-defined site and need substantial growth in the individual's and organization's capabilities in terms of technology, qualified personnel, facilities, and financial resources.

Although it is widely acknowledged that CSA is an important element in the adaptation of agriculture to climate change [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib20),[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib21)], studies of the challenges and prospects of CSA adoption on implications for food security of smallholder farmers are lacking.

Furthermore, despite the benefits that CSA technologies promise, the uptake of CSA know-how among African farmers has been facing challenges as re-thinking in CSA increases and the changing aspects of many African cultural, socioeconomic, and environmental countries change [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib22)]. There is a particular need for robust studies that improve understanding of what works where and why in different agro-ecologies and [farming systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/farming-system "Learn more about farming systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), facilitating what constitutes ‘climate smartness’ in biophysical and socio-economic contexts. Therefore, this paper reviews the challenges and prospects of adopting CSA practices and technologies and its implication for food security in Sub-Saharan Africa.

2. Materials and methods
------------------------

This review used an integrative literature review method, as per described by Torraco, R.J.[23] and Snyder, H., [24]. Integrative literature review to address the mature or new and emerging topics. In this case, the specific purpose of the review paper always determines the right strategy to use. The review paper identified the challenges and opportunities for adopting [CSA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about CSA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") practices and technologies and the implications of [CSA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/climate-smart-agriculture "Learn more about CSA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for [food security](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/food-security "Learn more about food security from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in sub-Saharan African country.

### 2.1. Criteria for inclusion and exclusion

To find relevant information about the challenges and opportunities of adopting CSA practices and technologies and its implication for food security, the search keywords like ''climate-smart agriculture'' with ''food security'' and ''challenges and prospects'' with ''CSA″ were combined. ENDNOTE reference manager software version 20 was used to compile appropriate works of literature. [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#tbl1) lists the criteria for selecting suitable literature for the study.

Table 1. Criteria for including and excluding documents.

| Selection Procedures | Elimination Standards |
| --- | --- |
| Paper that is written in English | Documents written other than English |
| Documents such as Journal articles, reviews, or chapters of the book | File types that are not journal articles, reviews, as well as book chapters. |
| Studies focused on the farming area | Studies not focused on farming area |
| Talks at slightest single CSA principles (productivity, adaptation, and mitigation) | Reports that exclude CSA principles |
| Documents that contain suitable aspects to carry out data analysis | Papers that do not deliver the necessary elements undertake data examination |

Scopus Source, web of Science, Google Search, Google Scholar data bases and other developmental organization websites like Food and Agricultural Organization (FAO) and Consultative Group on International Agricultural Research (CGIAR) were used. Emphasis has been given to the documents that focus on pillars of CSA in sub-Saharan Africa (See [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#fig1)).

![Image 8: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666154323002053-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (610KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666154323002053-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (610KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666154323002053-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. PRISMA selection for identifying the included and excluded studies.

3. Result and discussion
------------------------

### 3.1. The implication of CSA for food security

The African agricultural system shows more vulnerability to changing climatic conditions when compared with the other regions [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib25)]. Specifically, Sub-Saharan Africa is not yet at the peak of [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); yet, past fluctuations in temperature and precipitation have a direct impact on food security and productivity across the continent [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib26)]. Flooding, heat stress, and famine have worsened the already inadequate crop, animal, and [aquatic species](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/aquatic-species "Learn more about aquatic species from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") output and raised rates of starvation and poverty, resulting in a lower quality of life and health [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib27),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib28)]. SSA will have the highest rates of hunger and poverty compared to other parts of the continent, making it harder to get food because of the rapid population growth [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib29)].

This review mainly focused on the challenges and prospects of adopting CSA practices and technologies and its implication for food security in Sub-Saharan Africa (See [Table 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#tbl2)). The reviewed studies reported that CSA practices and technologies adoption has a [sustainability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about sustainability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") impact on food security (changing plant variety, land rotation, mulching, and crop diversification [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib31)]. Nevertheless, their study lacks in showing the challenges and opportunities of CSA technologies and practices adoption. According to Sarker, Wu [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib32)], in the changing climatic conditions, CSA is an effective approach to enhance agricultural production and feed people across the world. However, the concept of CSA is still evolving, and there is limited research that explores the linkages between CSA and [sustainable agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/sustainable-agriculture "Learn more about sustainable agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and how those practices of CSA can be promoted to achieve food security and development goals.

Table 2. Summary of the findings from the review of the included studies.

| Author/s | Title of the Studies | Country | Major findings on the challenges and prospects of adopting CSA practices and technologies |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Arslan, A. et al. [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib23)] | Adaptation Implications of the Climate Smart Agriculture | Zambia | Farmer's adaptation to climate change varies by the types of climate impacts and agro-ecology region. |
| Dadzie, S.K.N. et al. [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib24)] | Sustainability responses to climate-smart adaptation in Africa: Implication for food security | Ghana | The sustainability of farm practices is being impacted by the farmers' choice of climate-smart adaptation measures at the farm level. |
| Onyeneke, R.U. et al., [[79](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib79)] | Status of climate-smart agriculture in southeast Nigeria | Nigeria | Education, income, credit, extension, livestock ownership, farming experience, land area cultivated, gender, land ownership, household size, and mass media exposure were determinants factors affecting CSA |
| Andrieu, N. et al. [[68](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib68)] | Prioritizing investments for climate-smart agriculture: Lessons learned from Mali | Mali | The implementation of prioritized practices in research and development programs and the request for support to mainstream CSA by the Ministry of Agriculture and the Parliament. |
| Were, K. et al., [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib27)] | Smart Strategies for Enhanced Agricultural Resilience and Food Security Under a Changing Climate in Sub-Saharan Africa, in Climate Change and Multi-Dimensional Sustainability in African Agriculture: Climate Change and Sustainability in Agriculture | SSA | No single CSA technology and practice offers a solution to the foregoing challenges and must be integrated when choosing the most suitable climate-smart technologies and practices for any given agro-ecology |
| Partey, S.T. et al. [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib28)] | Developing climate-smart agriculture to face climate variability in West Africa: Challenges and lessons learned. | West Africa | CSA still faces several challenges, including a lack of clear conceptual understanding, and limited enabling policy and financing. The prospects of CSA in West Africa hinge on the capacities of farming households and the region's national institutions to understand the environmental, economic, and social challenges |
| Zougmoré, R.B.et al., [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib29)] | Transforming Food Systems in Africa under Climate Change Pressure: Role of Climate-Smart Agriculture. | Ghana, Senegal, Mali, Burkina Faso, in West Africa, Ethiopia, Kenya, Rwanda, Tanzania and East Africa | CSA can contribute to driving a rapid change in food systems in Africa |
| Mango, N. et al., [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib30)] | Adoption of Small-Scale Irrigation Farming as a CSA Practice and Its Influence on Household Income | Southern Africa | Adoption of small-scale irrigation farming as a climate-smart agriculture practice has a significant positive influence on agricultural income. |

Despite the potentiality of attaining sustainable agricultural development goals by implementing CSA properly, there is a low linkage among the components of CSA at the field level. More importantly, the concept is sometimes poorly understood by various stages stakeholders. A better understanding of the concept of CSA, its linkages with sustainable agriculture, and the pathways to promote CSA will enable a more integrated climate change response and aider adoption of CSA practices towards attaining food security and [sustainable development goals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/sustainable-development-goals "Learn more about sustainable development goals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). As a result, it is critical to reinforce and disseminate CSA approaches at the field level to achieve a more sustainable and prosperous future [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib32)].

Eventhough, there were successful pilot projects, the uptake of new and innovative agricultural technologies and practices has often been poor, and we have still not been able to resolve food insecurity and rural poverty problems. Some studies, including those byWestermann, Förch [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib33)], stated that due to low adoption rates by farmers in developing countries, a variety of agricultural technologies and practices, including some that qualify as CSA, are not having the full impact they could be having. There is a need to show real impact beyond the plot or site level to impact more people over wider areas and on institutions and policies that drive interest in scaling up [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib38)]. Hasan, Desiere [[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib39)], revealed that CSA practices adoption was positively associated with household food security in terms of per capita annual food expenditure. Their study highlighted that increasing the adoption of CSA (especially effective use of [organic fertilizer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/organic-fertilizer "Learn more about organic fertilizer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and mulching) was crucial in enhancing food security through increased [food production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/food-production "Learn more about food production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Other researchers (see, for example, [[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib40),[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib41)], have found positive effects of CSA practices and technologies on input use efficiency, increased production, farm income, and reducing emerging environmental problems. According to Lipper _et al_. [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib16)], the current evidence base is inadequate to support effective decision-making and sufficiently inaccessible to decision-makers at the national and local levels. Moreover, although there are CSA technologies available in SSA, their utilization is still low. This confirms the report of Totin, and Roncoli [[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib43)], that CSA use by [smallholder](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/smallholder "Learn more about smallholder from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") farms presents an added opportunity to embark on rigorous promotional campaigns for effectiveness.

The African agricultural system shows more vulnerability to changing climatic conditions when compared with the other regions [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib44). Specifically, Sub-Saharan Africa is not yet at the peak of climate change; yet, past fluctuations in temperature and precipitation have a direct impact on food security and productivity across the continent [[45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib45)]]. Flood, heat stress, and famine have worsened the already inadequate crop, animal, and aquatic species output and raised rates of starvation and poverty, resulting in a lower quality of life and heal [[[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib27)]]. SSA will have the highest rates of hunger and poverty compared to other parts of the continent, making it very difficult to get food because of the rapid population grwh [[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib40)][]. CSA adoption, and that this could begin with the local media and through the agricultural [extension agents](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/extension-agents "Learn more about extension agents from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). # Communicating the Benefits and Risks of Digital Agriculture Technologies: Perspectives on the Future of Digital Agricultural Education and Training

![Tammara Soma
](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1450203/74)
![Bhoosun Nuckchady](https://loop.frontiersin.org/cdn/images/profile/default_32.jpg)

British Columbia’s food system is experiencing an emerging trend in the digitalization of agriculture, which will impact agricultural practices in the province. The rapid growth of this field has created a niche for training and education in digital agriculture and more specifically, in areas such as robotics, artificial intelligence, big data analytics, and computing. However, it remains unclear whether current educators and trainers in British Columbia are communicating both the benefits and risks of digital agriculture, and the need for an inclusive and equitable approach to digital agriculture. To understand the emerging education and training landscape in digital agricultural technologies, this exploratory study engaged in a key informant interview with 12 participants, including educators, relevant government staff, and private training consultants/practitioners in the food and agricultural sector in British Columbia. The small sample is reflective of the nascent nature of this area of research, which seeks to better understand digital agriculture from the perspectives of agricultural educators and trainers both in the public and private sectors. The study found that there is currently a lack of consideration for equity and food sovereignty in digital agricultural training and education. This is primarily due to a gap in engagement with the social aspects of digital agriculture. Without engaging critical social scientists and critical data studies, digital agriculture education, and training may be conducted in ways that do not promote responsible and ethical innovation, and are therefore counterproductive to the development of a just and sustainable food system.

## 1 Introduction

For decades, the agriculture sector in Canada has been undergoing a trend toward a digital agricultural revolution. The promise of digital agricultural technology is that it presents an opportunity for improved productivity and environmental benefits through more efficient use of natural resources ([Newell and Taylor, 2018](#B48); [Rose and Chilvers, 2018](#B55)). Some of the claimed benefits of digital agriculture are increased yields with fewer inputs, greater environmental stewardship, and social benefits such as less manual labour on farms ([Edwards et al., 2020](#B20)). On the other hand, there are also documented concerns. [Weersink (2018)](#B72) argues that digitization has led to the decrease in numbers of “average-sized farms” in Canada and a subsequent increase in large farms due to technological innovations that accelerate production operations. Older farmers and rural farming communities could be excluded from this agricultural digital revolution due to a lack of accessible training and internet connectivity. Farmers might not always understand the data obtained from digital devices and there are also documented issues of trust with respect to data ownership and privacy, as well as a growing digital divide ([Rotz et al., 2019](#B56); [Weersink, 2018](#B72); [Bronson and Knezevic, 2019](#B6)). With the development of more advanced technologies such as wireless communication, big-data analytics, cloud-based storage, and data-driven genomes, this data-driven farming requires specific skills and training. The application of digital agricultural technologies and associated training must consider potential harm, farmers’ concerns, as well as ensure equity considerations and the sharing of benefits from the technology ([Wield et al., 2010](#B74); [Wiseman et al., 2019](#B75)).

The Government of Canada has invested around $49.5 million to engage agricultural stakeholders from the different provinces in the development of digital agricultural tools ([Brunner, 2019](#B10)). For example, one government program—with a $3 billion budget (2018–2023)—that aims to increase agricultural outputs using new agricultural technological innovations is the Canadian Agricultural Partnership ([Government of Canada, 2020](#B30)). Such financial incentives provide an immense opportunity for Canadian provinces, including British Columbia, to develop their Agri-tech sector. In July 2019, the Government of British Columbia set up the Food Security Task Force to investigate the benefits and costs of digitizing province’s food system ([Government of British Columbia, 2020](#B28)). The findings of the task force identified digital agriculture as the way forward for a sustainable food system which reduces food insecurity and GHG emissions. Some scholars have also argued that such an initiative will lead to an improvement of internet infrastructure to support an increasing range of agricultural activities and countless benefits in rural areas ([Bolfe et al., 2020](#B5)). Another key recommendation by the task force members emphasized the creation of Agri-tech institutions that would facilitate the development of digital agricultural technologies and the training of farmers and students in using these tools ([Government of British Columbia, 2020](#B28)). However, some scholars argue in response to the task force that the focus on technology as a means to achieve food security and sustainable food systems is insufficient and calls for a more cautious approach to the role of agri-tech ([Hansen et al., 2020](#B31)).

It is within this emerging and rapidly changing landscape in both the agricultural sector and agri-food focused institutions that this study will highlight what may currently be a “niche” role of educators and trainers in digital agriculture in British Columbia. Due to what are often polarizing perspectives and communications around the potential risks and benefits of digital agriculture, it is critical to better understand the perspectives of agricultural educators and trainers on how they approach the issue of digital agriculture and identify potential gaps that have not been considered. Moreover, it is important to understand whether or not equity considerations and some of the concerns around the technology are being taken into account in digital agriculture education and training.

With the growing interest in digital agriculture and balancing both the potential benefits and risks around digital agriculture, this study seeks to provide insights into the current trend and the future of digitization within British Columbia’s agriculture education/training sector, including in post-secondary institutions, in the public (via government provided resources and training), and in the private sector (via consultants). The study seeks to address the following research objectives: 1) To explore educators’/trainers’ perspectives and approaches to the benefits and risks of digital agricultural technologies in their education, communication, and training; 2) To assess whether digital agriculture training and or pedagogy includes/consider outcomes such as social equity and food sovereignty; and 3) To identify appropriate policies to promote an ethical and responsible approach to digital agriculture in education and training. By engaging with 12 educators, trainers, and government staff who are involved in providing agriculture and digital agriculture training in the province of British Columbia, Canada, the findings from this study contribute to efforts to better prepare farmers, educators, and students for emerging trends in digital agriculture. Moreover, a better understanding of educators’ perspectives/discourse on digital agriculture can help identify the skills and mindset needed to ensure that learners are exposed to a holistic understanding of both the benefits and the potential limitations of digital agriculture. In this study, a discourse analysis ([Paltridge, 2021](#B51)) will be applied to understand the dominant discourse that is framing digital agriculture training and education, particularly who benefits, and who bears the burden of risk and liability. Discourse analysis can be helpful with respect to understanding how spoken and written words contribute to the construction of certain views of the world ([Paltridge, 2021](#B51)), which in this study is based on how digital agriculture is portrayed by educators and trainers.

## 2 Literature Review

### 2.1 Digital Agricultural Revolution: Cause for Celebration or for Concern?

There has been an increasing call for the sustainable intensification of agriculture to reduce the carbon footprint of agricultural activities, increase food production, and improve the economic conditions of the farming community ([Lowder et al., 2016](#B41); [Firbank et al., 2018](#B22)). Framing this “fourth agricultural revolution” as “smart agriculture,” and “digital agriculture”, public discourse and the media have promoted it as the technological fix of future agricultural and food system challenges ([Van der Burg et al., 2019](#B70)). Digital agriculture may take place in the adoption and use of new technologies, the use of advanced sensor capability, improved data connectivity, and computer-based artificial or augmented intelligence (AI) decision support and self-learning systems ([Shepherd et al., 2020](#B61)). While it is a fact that these digital technologies will change the farming culture of communities and agricultural actors, it is still too early to determine how these are perceived by stakeholders and assess its impacts on the society ([Balafoutis et al., 2020](#B2)).

Precision agriculture focuses on the data generation process which involves on-the-field collection of data through mobile devices, field sensors and satellites. Digital agriculture, on the other hand, goes many steps further by connecting farm equipment to software platforms ([Mehrabi et al., 2021](#B45); [Clapp and Ruder, 2020](#B17)). The on-farm data is processed using deep learning algorithms and big stack data to enable the farmers or the company to view all production parameters of real-time operations and provide advice regarding seed choice or application of fertilizer and pesticides ([Ozdogan et al., 2017](#B50); [Clapp and Ruder, 2020](#B17)). These types of tools and information were once exclusively the domain of agricultural extension services but can technically complement or support extension-related work.

Proponents of digital agriculture argue that the use of technology simplifies the complexity of agricultural activities as more detailed and precise data is available to support complex decision-making on-farm; enabling the move “from precision to decision” ([Shepherd et al., 2020](#B61), pp. 5083). It is claimed that such precise agricultural data paves the way for farmers to be in line with environmental standards and regulation that are becoming more stringent worldwide ([Saunders et al., 2016](#B57)). Furthermore, on a global scale, scholars argue that these digital technologies will provide greater market access for agricultural products as more information about the farm producing the food will create more transparency and traceability through the use of verifiable records and labelling in complex food supply chains ([Shepherd et al., 2020](#B61)).

Some scholars note that the digitization of agriculture would lead to increased production for fewer inputs and a reduction in toxins from agrochemical use due to more precise chemical applications ([Basso and Antle, 2020](#B3); [Shepherd et al., 2020](#B61)). This phenomenon, in which more food is produced on less land and with less input, is claimed to result in fewer environmental impacts but also increases farm receipt ([Garnett and Godfray, 2012](#B26)). Greater efficiency and productivity are the main arguments of proponents of digital agriculture as it balances the socio-economic and the environmental aspects of sustainable agriculture and the food system ([Basso and Antle, 2020](#B3)).

However, there are also barriers identified by scholars about digital agriculture. For example, internet access in both urban and rural communities is critical to the uptake of digital agriculture and the use of Big Data analytics platforms ([Weersink, 2018](#B72)). Even in 2021, many rural areas of North America and Europe experience a lack of broadband and internet access because internet service providers do not generate the same profit margin as in large cities where they have millions of customers ([Pant and Odame, 2017](#B53)). Moreover, the average age of farmers in Western countries is viewed as a barrier to digital technology as increasing age has a negative correlation with the adoption of technological tools such as computers and online platforms ([Tey and Brindal, 2012](#B68)).

Beyond barriers to adoption, there are also larger concerns around the use of the technology itself and its potential negative impact on farmers. Often, farmers are captured by sales pitches about a specific precision or digital agricultural system, which can make them believe that acquiring that technology will help them increase crop yield or better manage farm issues. However, the ways in which digital tools are marketed, combined with other powerful social forces, trap many farmers in technological lock-in via debt ([McKinnon, 2019](#B43)). Debt is a key mechanism that farmers engage in to acquire an agricultural technology of a specific brand and system ([McMichael, 2013](#B44); [Rotz et al., 2019](#B56)). Unfortunately, these agricultural systems are sometimes unable to process agricultural data that come from digital tools of other brands, and this not only reinforces the societal dominance of certain technological systems over others, it also leaves the farmer with financial debt and limited data processing capabilities ([Rotz et al., 2019](#B56); [McKinnon, 2019](#B43); [McMichael, 2013](#B44)). Additionally, the dependence of farmers on digital software to guide their farming practices reinforces the technological lock-in as farmers lose their traditional way of evaluating trade-offs and may be unable to fix their machinery or perform agriculture without digital support ([Carolan M. S., 2017](#B15); [Rotz et al., 2019](#B56)).

While digital agriculture promises environmental sustainability, negative environmental consequences can also happen if the technology is not utilized in the safest way due to a lack of strict regulations. Another major concern is the issue of data ownership. Land grabs in the twenty-first century depend on digital knowledge and needs agricultural data. From a digital agricultural technology provider’s standpoint, land represents a block of data and digital agriculture acts as facilitator to capture information about micro-scale qualities of land and lives which are inputted into a data analytics infrastructure operated by multi-national corporations ([Fraser, 2019](#B25)). Such large vertically integrated and multinational enterprises have the data analytic platforms to evaluate the inputted on-farm data ([Clapp and Ruder, 2020](#B17); [Weersink, 2018](#B72)). Even though farmers agree to the terms and conditions of using digital agriculture platforms, they have little influence in determining consent rights to their data as agricultural companies remain unclear data ownership and whether the data is used for other purposes, such as data sharing agreements with third parties ([Custers, 2016](#B18); [Wiseman et al., 2019](#B75)). The collection of agricultural data and gene editing by these large agricultural companies make farmers more hesitant to share their data as they fear an agricultural research agenda that will cause economic and environmental consequences ([Clapp and Ruder, 2020](#B17)). Corporate-controlled data analytics platforms are indeed not the most appropriate medium to restore equity among the different agricultural stakeholders and safeguard the privacy and livelihood of small farmers, who feel excluded from the value chain ([Weersink, 2018](#B72)). Sadly, such approach with digital agriculture will continue to widen the profitability gap between marginalized small farmers and multi-national companies.

### 2.2 Digitization of Agriculture in Canada

When it comes to Digital Agriculture, the Federal Government of Canada is actively engaging the different provinces, education and research institutions, large agribusiness companies and small enterprises throughout the country. With a view to becoming a global leader in digital technologies for food and agriculture, the federal government has launched the Canadian Agri-Food Automation and Intelligence Network (CAAIN) to regroup 61 technology and agri-food companies including eight core partners such as Alberta Innovates, the Vineland Research and Innovation Centre, Olds College, MDA Systems Ltd., Linamar Corp., Lakeland College, DOT Technology Corporation, and TrustBIX share in the $49.5 million contributions from the Strategic Innovation Fund ([Brunner, 2019](#B10)). Additional funding of $15 million will be allocated to other small and medium food enterprise partners to be able to work on an automation and digital technology project to highlight the economic benefits and impacts of digitization of the Agri-Food sector (Morin, 2020). Another federal and provincial program which aims to increase agricultural outputs using new agricultural innovations is the Canadian Agricultural Partnership with a $3 billion budget (2018–2023) in the agriculture sector. The Canadian Agricultural Partnership is cost-shared between the federal and provincial/territorial governments with the federal government contributing 60% of the costs of the program and the provincial/territorial government contributing 40% ([Government of Canada, 2020](#B30)). This funding is beneficial for start-up, agri-food tech companies involved in a wide range of digital agriculture activities such as farm management platforms, the Internet of Things and novel farming techniques ([Schmaltz, 2019](#B59)).

The Government of Canada is setting the path for the gradual digitization of the agriculture and agri-food sector by providing financial incentives and promoting collaboration between agricultural and technology stakeholders more broadly, as well as in academia. A growing number of start-up agri-food tech companies are also collaborating with the Government of Canada as well as academic institutions ([Government of Canada, 2018](#B29)). The fact that industry (including small and medium-sized enterprises and consultancies), post-secondary institutions, research institutes, and non-profit organizations from multiple sectors across Canada are working with the government in the development of digital agricultural platforms outlines the importance of understanding the nuances of communications particularly around the benefits and limitations of digital agriculture.

### 2.3 Agri-Food Systems Education and Training

In 1870, John Carling, Ontario’s Commissioner of Public Works and Agriculture announced that Canada needed agricultural education in “the science of farming” ([Lawr, 1972](#B37), 334). The growth of nineteenth-century determinism and the intellectual environment that set the precedence of agricultural education is encompassed in the terminology of “scientific agriculture” with a popular expression at that time based on the idea that “the minds of the agricultural should be irradiated with the beams of science” ([Lawr, 1972](#B37), 335). Early agricultural education at the University of Toronto in the 1850s saw students take courses in Agricultural Chemistry, Comparative Physiology, Mineralogy, Geography, Surveying, Botany, Management of Property, and Farm Finance ([Lawr, 1972](#B37)). Compared to Canadian agricultural education, the American agricultural education in agricultural colleges flourished with significant public aid and land grants. The Canadian agricultural and education system was very much influenced by the success of agricultural colleges in the United States and the experimental farm model imported from British agricultural training. Reverend Clarke, a representative from Canada and rector of the first agricultural school in Ontario was sent to the United States to study the agricultural school model there. The earliest agricultural school (Ontario School of Agriculture) supported by the government had the hope of not only teaching good farming but also encouraging rural youth to take a farming vocation instead of leaving for the city. In his words, the school should “urge the importance of a higher standard of mental culture and a general uplifting of that noblest and yet most despised of human pursuits, Life on a Farm.” (Ontario, 1871, 15 as cited in [Lawr, 1972](#B37)). Some who supported the school noted that “dirty hands” is not a necessary virtue in a farmer (Farmers’ Advocate, 1877 as cited in [Lawr, 1972](#B37)).

In the 19th century, the science of genetics, animal and plant nutrition, and animal biology was non-existent or still in its infancy. As such, it took after the first world war for new scientific developments on vaccinations, soil testing, genetics that would support the pursuits of agricultural science ([Lawr, 1972](#B37)). Eventually, a key success of agricultural education and training programs were through the provision of local and extension support in arranging crop competitions, short courses, demonstration plots, and providing direct assistance for farmers. College-trained agricultural graduates of Ontario Agricultural College (OAC) became in demand and their expertise not only filled federal and provincial agriculture departments, their expertise was also exported to other countries ([Lawr, 1972](#B37)). In a nutshell, the history of agricultural training in Canada from 19th to the 20th century primarily focused on supporting vocational agricultural programs for the purpose of extension services and on teaching the economics of agriculture (e.g Agricultural Economics) as well as natural science aspects pertaining to agriculture (e.g plant science, soil science, chemistry, veterinary science, and engineering etc). The science focus was done to avoid the stigma of rural life and dirty work on the farm. The extension principles and methods of agriculture developed in North America and Canada were also transplanted in the global South to promote a particular approach of “scientific agriculture” to food production and agricultural development ([Gill, 1996](#B27), 3).

However, the social aspects of agriculture, including the impact of agricultural practices on society do not traditionally enter into mainstream agricultural training or courses. In the emphasis on scientific agriculture, there has been a gap in considering the social aspects of agriculture. The social consideration of the impact of agriculture is traditionally housed under social science fields such as food studies, sociology and anthropology, and more recently in food systems. Previously, there were very limited avenues to study food systems as a whole ([Hilimire et al., 2014](#B32)). In recent years, however, the number of university programs and food systems related courses increased as agricultural, environmental, and food systems related issues became more prevalent in urban and rural settings ([Hilimire et al., 2014](#B32); [Levkoe et al., 2020](#B38)). Degrading soil and water qualities, environmental pollution, food insecurity, and accessibility, worsening labour conditions, concerns around food justice, and a changing climate due to industrial agricultural practices began to draw the attention of academia and the civil society in the whole socio-economic and environmental impacts of food systems ([Hilimire et al., 2014](#B32); [Francis et al., 2003](#B24)). There are growing university and community partnerships around food systems, and an expansion of online food systems-related course offerings ([Levkoe et al., 2020](#B38)). A shift has also occurred in that agri-food system professional, and farmers increasingly need to understand global and local food systems as a whole and recognize the interconnectedness of human and natural systems ([Liu et al., 2015](#B39)). Food literacy education is also shifting beyond nutrition and food preparation skills to increase students’ awareness about sustainable food production methods, the need to value the role of farmers, animal welfare, and the socio-economic and political factors shaping the food system ([Sumner, 2015](#B67)).

Within this context, farmers and agri-food professionals will need to develop the skills to deal with the dynamics, complexity, and uncertainty of the different processes in the food value chain ([Shulman, 2005](#B62); [Valley et al., 2018](#B69)). As the rise of digital agriculture requires professionals who can analyze agricultural on-farm data and operate these new emerging technologies, post-secondary institutions will play an increasingly important role in preparing students, and future agri-food educators, trainers, and farmers for a world where digitization is more pervasive. However, post-secondary institutions are not the only spaces for farmer education and training as farmers may receive agricultural education training from extension services offered by the government, and private consultants. There is also a long history of alternative ways of knowing, sharing, and dissemination of agricultural knowledge both formal and non-formal approaches ([Mars and Ball, 2016](#B42)). For example, community-based extensions support benefits from the social capital of the local communities whether it be “fee-for-service” or free extension support organized by farmers ([Yamada et al., 2015](#B77)).

While historically there has been limited emphasis on food systems education programs that focus on complex food-system issues such as food sustainability, security, quality, and justice, more courses and programs have been established in several North American universities in the past decade ([Jordan et al., 2014](#B33)). Food justice and food equity studies have grown with the increased understanding that developing an agri-food system that is sustainable requires a holistic approach that considers environmental, social, and economic considerations ([Migliorini et al., 2020](#B46)). Equity in the food system also entails consideration of intersectionality and anti-oppression, particularly with respect to race, gender, class, and other factors that may result in inequalities ([Sbicca, 2012](#B58)). Concerns around inequity in the food system are addressed in the various works of social scientists ranging from the lack of justice in migrant labour ([Weiler et al., 2017](#B73)), to inequities in food access for Indigenous peoples ([Skinner et al., 2013](#B64)) and more. While digital agriculture is covered extensively in natural science and technical science literature, in social science, there is only a scattered and emerging body of work investigating the social aspects of digital agriculture ([Klerkx et al., 2019](#B34)). [Carolan (2018)](#B12) found it surprising that social scientists have only recently started to explore what the Big Data revolution in agriculture will mean for farmers and food futures.

### 2.4 Discourse Analysis of Agri-Food Educators and Trainers

Currently, there is little in the literature to help students or farmers interested in digital agriculture to better understand the nuances (opportunities, risk, and limitations) and the implications of a food system that is highly digitized. As this field is still emerging, agri-food system practitioners, farmers, educators, and students need to start thinking seriously about digital agriculture as it is becoming a major component of the food system and as noted above, there are implications in how digital agriculture may impact everything from land use to food production, data collection, resource management, and more. An understanding of digital agriculture is key to prepare future food system professionals to better deal with its impact, opportunities, and its potential unintended consequences. Discourse analysis is a useful tool in understanding the framing of digital agriculture from the perspectives of educators and trainers that will impart training in this field. The method has been used to understand the perspectives of agricultural extension service workers and improve extension practice accordingly ([Fleming and Vanclay, 2009](#B23)). While the term discourse analysis is used in different ways by scholars in diverse fields ([Fairclough, 2003](#B78)), according to [Paltridge (2021)](#B51), discourse analysis examines patterns of spoken and written language including the relationships between language and the broader social/cultural contexts. It also considers how worldviews and identities are constructed through the use of discourse ([Paltridge, 2021](#B51)). In his approach to discourse analysis, [Sinclair (2004)](#B63) argues that analysis of meaning in discourse should be the key focus. Scholars working in Foucauldian discourse analysis are particularly interested in analyzing power ([Cheek, 2004](#B16)).

Unpacking power and meaning through discourse analysis in the context of digital agriculture is important. The historical context of agricul

enriched_points.expanded_content: Farmers often hesitate to invest in smart barn technologies due to unclear financial benefits and potential risks associated with initial costs and ongoing maintenance.
Traditional farming practices create a strong reluctance among farmers to adopt new technologies, fearing disruption of established routines and skepticism about their effectiveness.
Effective adoption of smart barn systems requires comprehensive education and training programs to ensure farmers understand technology benefits and how to utilize them effectively.
